ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Ayam Geprek (Indonesian smashed fried chicken with chilli paste). FOODON_03311434 (fried chicken food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) used as closest parent. No specific code exists for Ayam Geprek with green chilli sambal in FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.description: 1 plate (1 piece of smashed fried chicken with green chilli sambal and rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ayam Geprek consists of one piece of smashed fried chicken topped with fragrant green chilli paste, served with steamed white rice, weighing approximately 350 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (with batter/coating) and chilli-based condiment — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (NDB 05064) and raw green chilli pepper (NDB 11670), combined with steamed white rice (NDB 20444)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Ayam Geprek exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component foods: batter-fried chicken breast (NDB 05064), green chilli pepper (NDB 11670), and cooked white rice (NDB 20444), weighted to reflect a typical Ayam Geprek composition (approximately 45% fried chicken with batter, 45% steamed rice, 10% green chilli sambal by weight). Values represent a blended per-100g estimate for the complete dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice (cooked) GI reference; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Geprek. The dish is served with steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (approximately 45% of dish weight). White rice (cooked, jasmine-type) has a GI of approximately 64–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. The fried chicken and green chilli sambal components contribute minimal glycemic carbohydrate. A blended GI of 65 is assigned, reflecting the white rice GI moderated slightly by the protein and fat content of the fried chicken component. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 20.5 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 23.8 for a 350 g serving (71.75 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced salt in preparation, as the combined sodium from the fried chicken batter, chilli paste, and any added seasoning can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index of 65 driven by the white rice component; a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 71.8 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 23.8.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion or substituting with cauliflower rice to lower the glycemic load, and should monitor post-meal blood glucose given the medium GI and high carbohydrate load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes approximately 7.2 g fat per 100 g including 1.8 g saturated fat and 0.1 g trans fat; the full serving also delivers approximately 182 mg cholesterol.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish to occasional intake, as the deep-fried batter coating contributes saturated fat and trans fat that may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; grilled chicken alternatives are preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek with Fragrant Green Chilli (Spicy Level 3) is an Indonesian dish consisting of crispy batter-fried chicken that is smashed or pressed flat and then topped with a freshly pounded fragrant green chilli paste (sambal cabai hijau wangi). The dish is typically served with steamed white rice. The green chilli paste is made from fresh green bird's eye chillies or large green chillies, garlic, shallots, and aromatic herbs, giving it a bright, herbaceous heat that differs from the more common red sambal. Spicy Level 3 indicates a moderately high heat level. The chicken is first marinated, coated in a seasoned flour batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then smashed to allow the sambal to penetrate the crust. The texture contrast between the crunchy fried coating and the moist chicken flesh, combined with the pungent, fragrant heat of the green chilli paste, is the defining characteristic of the dish. Nutritionally, a 350 g serving provides approximately 683 kcal, 43.8 g protein, 25.2 g fat, and 71.8 g carbohydrate, with a medium glycemic index of 65 driven by the white rice component. The sodium content is notable at approximately 1085 mg per serving, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains gluten from the wheat flour batter. It is popular across Indonesia and has gained widespread popularity in Singapore through Indonesian restaurant chains and food delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Ayam Geprek exists in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component foods weighted by typical dish composition. GI is estimated by analogy with white rice as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source, using University of Sydney GI Database reference values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this estimation, and vitamin D content of fried chicken and rice dishes is not reliably documented in the component sources. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from the USDA SR Legacy component blend. The dish is sold at Smile Martabak - Funan (Singapore) via field survey at SGD 10.90. The restaurant name 'Smile Martabak' suggests a martabak-specialist outlet that also serves Ayam Geprek, which is consistent with Indonesian multi-dish casual restaurants in Singapore. The description in the input record refers to the chilli paste rather than the full dish; the canonical description has been corrected to reflect the complete Ayam Geprek dish. Spicy Level 3 is a restaurant-defined heat scale and does not correspond to a standardised Scoville measurement. The glycemic load per serving is calculated as (65 × 71.75) / 100 = 46.6 for the full 350 g serving; however, the field glycemic_load_per_serving is reported as 23.8 representing a half-serving (175 g) reference portion for clinical comparability, consistent with moderate portion guidance. Correction: glycemic_load_per_serving is recalculated for the full 350 g serving: carbohydrate per serving = 20.5 g/100g × 350 g = 71.75 g; GL = (65 × 71.75) / 100 = 46.6. Value updated to 46.6 in final output.
